topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee in my mug felt too hot, a sudden, sharp jolt that made me grip the ceramic tighter. I watched Liam across the crowded cafe, his easy laughter directed entirely at Sarah. Every time he turned his head, his gaze swept past me, completely unfocused, like he were looking through me to some distant horizon. My pulse hammered a quick, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I tried to force a smile, a gesture that felt brittle on my face, but it dissolved before it reached my eyes. A strange, hollow ache settled in my chest, heavy and undeniable. When he finally did glance my way, it was a fleeting flicker, a momentary blankness, and then he was absorbed back into his conversation. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of wood on tile sounding unnaturally loud in the sudden quiet of my own head.

[story 2]

I kept running my thumb over the worn edge of the photograph tucked into my wallet. The edges were softened with handling, the colors faded, but the faces—mine and Liam’s—were undeniably clear. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I found myself scanning the room constantly, cataloging every interaction, searching for a sign, a shared glance, anything that would confirm the reality I was desperately trying to reject. Liam approached, his expression warm, the kind of easy familiarity that used to anchor me. He leaned in, ready to share some inside joke. But when he spoke, his eyes drifted over my shoulder, landing on a stranger. The air seemed to thin around me. I felt a coldness bloom in my stomach, a sudden, profound sense of being utterly unseen, like a ghost in a crowded room.

[story 3]

My fingers drummed a restless tattoo on the tabletop. The silence in the apartment was oppressive, amplifying the sound of my own breathing. I reread the old text messages, the shared memories flooding back—the scraped knees, the whispered secrets under the covers. Each recollection was a sharp, painful intrusion. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room three times before stopping dead by the window. Outside, the city lights blurred into streaks of indifferent color. I looked down at my hands, flexing my fingers until the skin felt tight and almost painful. A wave of profound disorientation washed over me, a sickening realization that the foundation of that shared history, the very core of my connection to Liam, might be entirely illusory.
